texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Global warming is one of the most serious problems facing the earth at present. Anthropogenic CO2 is one of the gases causing this problem, and its main sources are fossil fuel based power production (Desideri and Paolucci, 1999), traffic, iron, steel and cement production, and ferro-alloy production based on reduction with carbon. CO2 absorption in aqueous amine solutions is the most widely used capture process in the industry and the most viable at present. However, capital and operating costs are high, and in particular the heat required for absorbent regeneration. In addition, many of the amines themselves can be environmentally harmful (Calamari et al., 1980). The main objective of this work was to find a combination of amines which could combine satisfactory ecotoxicity and biodegradability properties, see Eide-Hagumo et al. (2009), and at the same time could perform well as a solvent for post-combustion CO2 capture. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, experimental data and a simplified vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) model for the absorption of CO2 into aqueous solutions of 3-methylaminopropylamine ( N -methyl-1,3-propane diamine - MAPA) and dimethyl-monoethanolamine (2-dimethylaminoethanol - DMMEA) are reported. The chemicals were selected based on kinetic, energy and environmental properties for post-combustion CO2 capture. The effect of the DMMEA/MAPA ratio and the total concentration level of amine were studied. Based on the results of preliminary semi-quantitative tests (screening) the best amine ratios were determined. Two amine concentrations were selected for the VLE study. The result of both the screening and the VLE measurements are compared to the generally used 5 M (30 mass %) monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The presented systems seem to be better performing than 5 M MEA, and have significantly higher cyclic capacity combined with moderate impact on the environment.
texts.text: In this work, experimental data and a simplified vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) model for the absorption of CO2 into aqueous solutions of 3-methylaminopropylamine ( N -methyl-1,3-propane diamine - MAPA) and dimethyl-monoethanolamine (2-dimethylaminoethanol - DMMEA) are reported. The chemicals were selected based on kinetic, energy and environmental properties for post-combustion CO2 capture. The effect of the DMMEA/MAPA ratio and the total concentration level of amine were studied. Based on the results of preliminary semi-quantitative tests (screening) the best amine ratios were determined. Two amine concentrations were selected for the VLE study. The result of both the screening and the VLE measurements are compared to the generally used 5 M (30 mass %) monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The presented systems seem to be better performing than 5 M MEA, and have significantly higher cyclic capacity combined with moderate impact on the environment.

texts.orig: amines individually. Henni et al. (2008) found that the tertiary amine DMMEA has lower reaction rate with CO2 than the primary amine MEA, but higher than the tertiary amine MDEA. Its dissociation constant was measured by Hamborg and Versteeg (2009) and Littel et al. (1990), and calculated by da Silva and Svendsen (2003). Physical solubility of CO2, determined with the N2O analogy, was determinded by Versteeg and van Swaaij (1988). The vapor pressure of pure MAPA and its aqueous solutions was measured by Kim et al. (2008), and according to Bru ´ der and Svendsen (2009) the DMMEA/MAPA systems can have faster kinetics and higher CO2 capacity then 5 M MEA. They also showed that the higher the DMMEA/MAPA ratio the higher cyclic capacity per kg solution. In the first part of this article the selection of the solvents is described. The VLE studies are described in the second part. In the third part a parameterized mathematical model is shown which can predict the CO2 partial pressure as function of temperature and loading. From the model, the heat of absorption can be also predicted.

texts.text: 2.1. The screening apparatus

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a comparison of absorption rate and loading capacity, a screening apparatus was used (Aronu et al., 2009; Bru ´ der et al., 2011). The new systems were compared to a 30 mass % (5 M) monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The absorption tests were performed at 40 1 C. The flows of N2 and CO2 were controlled by flow controllers (Bronkhorst High Tech 7 0.1% fullscale), and these also controlled the mixing ratio. The CO2 flow was set to 0.5 and the N2 to 4.5 Nl/min. This gave a 10% CO2 concentration in the inlet gas flow through the amine solution. The volume of absorbent solution was 750 ml. The CO2 content in the outlet gas was measured by an IR detector (Hartmann & Braun Uras 3G, o 1%) and continuously logged by LabView software (Ver 6.0). An analyzer calibration run was performed before every screening test, and the two points used were pure N2 and 10% CO2. The program then registered the measured remaining CO2 in the gas stream, and the difference between 0.5 Nl/min and the registered value by the IR instrument was saved as a function of time. The measurements were stopped when the measured outlet gas CO2 concentration reached 95% of the initial value. After the end of the absorption, a liquid sample was taken for measuring density and CO2 concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experiments were performed by pumping 150 ml of unloaded solution into the small vessel. When the set point temperature was reached, the desired pressure was adjusted on the gas flask reduction valve, and the rotation started. After one hour the CO2 feed was closed and 2-3 h were used for establishing and stabilizing equilibrium. The time needed was indicated by a stable system pressure and temperature. A liquid sample was then withdrawn to measure the CO2 loading. For this purpose a closed metal cylinder with known weight of unloaded amine solution was used to avoid any CO2 loss. From the known dilution, the concentration of CO2 in the original solution was calculated. The CO2 partial pressure was calculated from the total pressure by subtraction of partial pressures of DMMEA, MAPA and H2O based on Raoult's law. The analysis of CO2 in the liquid phase was done in the same way for all three apparatuses, by the BaCl2 method.

texts.orig: In some cases, the ceramic sinter used in the reactor for bubble distribution was blocked. The absorption/desorption glass reactor is batch type, and between the two processes the heat-up time was usually 45-50 minutes. The sinter holes are quite small and the CO2 can react with amine during the heat-up process between 40 and 80 1 C. While no gas is circulating and if heating time is long, it could happen that the solution in the glass sinter holes obtained very high loading and high viscosity and could thereby block the way of the gas flow starting at 80 1 C. This could result in high back pressure and failure in performing desorption. This happened mainly in the cases of high MAPA and total amine concentration which in this case were 3 M/5 M and 4 M/4 M DMMEA/MAPA. The cyclic capacity (equal the useful capacity difference between the loadings at the end of the absorption and desorption, respectively) for these cases could therefore not be determined (Table 1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To ensure that the measurements were repeatable, the screening test of system 4 M DMMEA/4 M MAPA was repeated (Table 1). The relative concentration difference between the two parallel measurements (reproducibility) determined by both titration and directly from the screening measurement as cumulative absorption, was found to be � 1.2 and 1.3%, respectively. This was considered satisfactory for this purpose and shows that the methods are repeatable. However, the differences between the two methods (BaCl2 and mass balance) are about 10% (Table 1) for 4 M DMMEA/4 M MAPA. For all systems the differences between the two methods were in the range  6-13% and the BaCl2 titration method always gives the lower CO2 concentration. Part of this discrepancy could be explained by loss of water. However, the screening tests are only expected to be semi-quantitative and the agreement between the methods is found satisfactory for this purpose.

texts.orig: How to objectively compare the measured systems is not obvious. MAPA is a diamine and so contains twice the amount of CO2-active centers compared to MEA or DMMEA (Fig. 1). Because of this, a comparison based both on moles of amine (molecules) and the total amino group concentration (NH2) can be found in Fig. 5. The total (maximum absorption) and the cyclic capacity, measured between 40 and 80 1 C, per kilogram of solution are also given in the same figure. The latter may be the best consideration for the industry. In the two most concentrated solutions, where MAPA and total amine concentrations are the highest, it was not possible to measure desorption and so determine the cyclic capacity, as already mentioned. In these solutions the total capacity is the highest. The systems with high viscosity, high total amine concentation (8 M) were not considered for the VLE tests because of the missing cyclic capacity.

texts.orig: Aronu et al. (2009) measured the maximum loading and cyclic capacity for 5 M MEA and found 2.42 and 1.27 mol CO2/kg solution (0.518 and 0.271 mol CO2/mol amine) in measurements identical to the present ones. That concentration is somewhat higher than found in this work, but it is still lower than any of the DMMEA/MAPA systems measured here. Although the loading capacity of the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system is comparable to the AMP/PZ system (Bru ´ der et al., 2011), the cyclic capacity of DMMEA/MAPA seems to be just around 75% of that of the AMP/PZ system. The comparison of the kinetics of these systems measured by the screening equipment is considered to be semiquantitative, as the bubble distribution can vary somewhat as function of the density and viscosity of the solution and thereby influence the duration of the test (Ma'mun et al., 2007).

texts.orig: For further studies, two systems with optimal properties for the industry were selected, both with high total and cyclic capacities. The 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA was selected because although it has lower cyclic capacity than the 5 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system, it has slightly higher total CO2 load per kg solution and the amine concentration is significantly lower (Table 1 and Fig. 5). This lower DMMEA concentration can be advantageous in industrial operation because as a tertiary monoamine, it releases the CO2 easier and as it has only one NH2 group. By releasing CO2 it becomes more volatile because of the loss of ionic character, especially at higher temperatures. The industrial stripping temperature considered is 120 1 C. In industrial operation this could mean higher volatility losses and increased DMMEA consumption for the 5/2 system. The other system selected was the 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA system used as a system for comparison having low promoter concentration. It is expected that MAPA plays a significant role in the absorption rate as it has one primary and one secondary amine group and it is clear that the stripping will be more difficult than for DMMEA. Both selected systems have the same number of CO2 active centers as the NH2 concentration is 7 mol/l.

texts.orig: The mass transfer rates in the systems can be estimated from the actual absorption rate dependence on loading (Fig. 6). The maximum absorption rate measured was 0.5 Nl/min and this level could be upheld for higher CO2 loadings with only two of the measured systems (4 M DMMEA/4 M MAPA and 3 M DMMEA/ 5 M MAPA). Here, MAPA, which is the more reactive component due to its primary and secondary amino-groups, is in higher concentration. From the middle rate solutions, where the concentration of MAPA is 2 mol/l, the 5 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA has a slightly higher initial absorption rate than 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA, This could be due to the higher DMMEA concentration. From this result it is also clear that MAPA contributes mostly to the kinetics of the system, and that DMMEA, as a tertiary amine, is much slower. The 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system ends at a slightly higher loading, probably due to the higher concentration of DMMEA which has, relative to MAPA, a lower equilibrium loading. The rate for this system is also lower at high loading probably because of higher viscosity for the 5 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system, which reduces the mass transfer rate of CO2. The 5 M MEA starts at a bit lower rate and practically never exceeds the rates of the solutions with 2 M MAPA concentration. The end of the absorption for 5 M MEA seems to be close to its practical maximum (2.5 mol/l at density 1.07 g/cm 3 ). The least attractive performance was found for the systems 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA and 5 M DMMEA/2 M MEA. It seems that MAPA gives a faster absorption rate than MEA, but as these two systems have the same number of NH2 groups, the final loading is nearly the same. The maximum theoretical load of these two systems is  6 mol CO2/l solution, but only about 50-60% of this was reached. texts.orig: In order to utilize an amine blend effectively, it is important that it is as fully loaded as possible. The practical absorption rate can be evaluated by the slope of the absorption curves at high loadings. For the 5 M MEA solution it can be seen that this curve moves toward equilibrium through a very steep descent. This indicates that it is easy to reach high loadings or loadings close to the equilibrium. For amine blends consisting of one rapid and one slow amine, this is often not the case. However, both the selected systems are seen to approach equilibrium through relatively steep curves. Thus reaching high CO2 loadings should be possible with a reasonable column height.

texts.orig: In general, all the k i parameters are linear functions of the inverse thermodynamic temperature, but in some cases this simplifies to constant values. Comparisons between the experimental points and model curves are shown in Figs. 7-9. The fits for the DMMEA/MAPA systems are acceptable but not as good as in the case of 3 AMP/1.5 PZ (Bru ´ der et al., 2011) or the single amine 5 M MEA system. The deviations can be due to the different behaviour of the two amines at different temperatures and loadings. According to the maximum loading from screening (0.58 mol CO2/mol amine) and the cyclic capacity (0.32 mol CO2/mol amine at density 1.03 g/cm 3 ), the useful range seems to be 0.26-0.58 mol CO2/mol amine. In this range the fit is in good correlation with the measured data (Fig. 7). It should be emphasised that the model in Eq. (3) is only a parameterised fit of the data and has no thermodynamic significance, but can be very useful for industrial purposes.

texts.orig: The range of the model validity based on the experimental data is up to a loading of about 0.79 mol CO2/mol amine for 100 1 C and elevated pressures, and for atmospheric pressure is  0.7 for the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system. The limits for the 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA are about 0.53 mol CO2/mol amine at 100 1 C and elevated pressure and at atmospheric pressure around  0.58 mol CO2/mol amine. For the 30 mass % MEA systems the measurements go to about 0.57 mol CO2/mol amine at 100 1 C and about 0.5 at atmospheric pressure. From the data is obvious that the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system has lower CO2 pressure above the solution at every measured temperature at a certain loading. This is in line with the screening results and means that this system captures the CO2 more effectively at the same temperature than does the 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA system, due to the higher concentration of primary and secondary amino-groups (MAPA). However, for the CO2 release, also higher heat input needed. The practical cyclic capacity (useful for the industry) of the system can be estimated by estimating the difference in partial pressure between 40 1 C (absorption temperature) and 120 1 C (desorption temperature) for the maximum loading reached at atmospheric pressure.

texts.orig: To have an estimate of the accuracy of the calculated heat of absorption, which is very much dependent on the accuracy of the fitting just as on the data points, it is possible to compare to the 5 M MEA system where measured data exist. It is known that the heat of absorption in 5 M MEA is about 83-84 kJ/mol CO2 absorbed and that it is reasonably constant up to a loading of 0.5, after which it falls rapidly as the capacity of MEA vanishes (Kim and Svendsen, 2007). To reach a reasonable fit using the parameterized Eq. (3) for the 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA system was more difficult than for the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system. The last, interaction part was neglected and only D H 0reac could be determined from (6) and was found to be � 65.4 kJ/mol CO2 absorbed. This value is uncertain, but not unreasonable taking into account the values found for 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA in Table 4 and the fact that the 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA has much more of the tertiary amine DMMEA. The parameterized model seems to give better fit for the relatively simple MEA system and the AMP/PZ system, than for the DMMEA/MAPA systems. Comparing the other two systems is obvious from Table 4. The 5 M MEA system has slightly lower heat requirement for stripping than the 3 M DMMEA/ 2 M MAPA system. The opposite behavior was expected, but this occurs probably due to the fitting accuracy.

At higher temperature (120 1 C) the first points show very high relative error indicating that the absorption capacity at infinite dilution should be very different. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two blended amine system consisting of 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA and 5 M DMMEA/1 M MAPA, respectively have been investigated. Screening tests and VLE equilibrium measurements were performed. The tertiary amine DMMEA seems to contribute to the lower heat of absorption, whereas the MAPA dominates in the kinetics and in the total capacity of the solution. The cyclic capacity of the two systems seems to be comparable per kg of solution and it is significantly higher than for the 5 M MEA, but lower than for the AMP/PZ system. Precipitation formation was not observed at any of the measured combinations of the amines. The partial CO2 pressure at 120 1 C and maximum loading over the solution of 3 M DMMEA/ 2 M MAPA are a bit higher than for 5 M MEA, but the 5 M DMMEA/ 1 M MAPA system seems to have an advantage in this respect, which could mean lower stripping heat requirement. The estimation of the heat of reaction of 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system against the MEA does not show significant improvement but further investigations are ongoing. A significant advantage of the DMMEA/MAPA systems could be their weaker environmental impact.
